question.text: Scenario: Rajiv Kapoor is an investment manager evaluating two different approaches to investing in the Indian equity market. He manages a corpus of Rs. 50 crore and is considering the following options: (1) Strategy A — Active Fundamental Approach: Hire a team of 3 research analysts to conduct bottom-up fundamental analysis, visiting companies, building financial models, and selecting 25-30 undervalued stocks. Expected expense ratio: 2.5% per annum. Historical outperformance vs Nifty 50: 3% per annum over the last 5 years. (2) Strategy B — Passive Index Approach: Invest in a Nifty 50 index fund with an expense ratio of 0.1% per annum. Expected return: Nifty 50 return minus tracking error of 0.05%. (3) Additionally, he has access to a quantitative model that uses regression analysis to predict quarterly earnings of Nifty 50 companies. The model has shown 65% accuracy in predicting the direction of earnings surprises over the last 3 years. Rajiv is in his 40s and has a 15-year investment horizon for this corpus which is part of a pension fund. Question 4 of 5: A limitation of the quantitative model that Rajiv should be aware of is:

answer.text: It produces results that are too accurate
It can capture all qualitative factors about a company
Changes in accounting standards or business models may reduce the reliability of historical patterns
It requires no human judgment or oversight

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A major limitation of quantitative research is that frequent changes in accounting standards and business models makes past data less useful for comparison with present conditions, reducing model reliability over time. **The Trap:** Quantitative models are not too accurate (35% error rate here), cannot capture qualitative factors, and still require human judgment for interpretation and model updates. **Cross-Exam Context:** Limitations of quantitative-only approaches are highlighted in NISM Series XV and X-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Models should be regularly backtested and recalibrated. A model built on 3 years of data may not perform well when accounting standards change (e.g., transition to IndAS) or business models shift.
